coredata.dc:title: ROS-activated selective fluorescence imaging of cancer cells via the typical LDH-based nanozyme
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Zhengdi
Liu, Jiancheng
Feng, Min
Song, Kai
Li, Kaitao
Liu, Wendi
Guan, Shanyue
Lin, Yanjun
coredata.dc:description: The detection and treatment based on tumor microenvironment (TME) are essential in cancer therapy. Currently, nanomaterials with mimic-enzyme property have attracted tremendous interest and the regulation of the sensing and catalytical activity inside the living cells remains a challenge. Herein, a reactive oxygen species (ROS) driven nanozyme, with selective activated fluorescence imaging and multiple enzyme activity (including peroxidase, superoxide dismutase and catalase), was achieved via the confinement of CDs on the ultrathin CuAl-LDH (denoted as CDs/LDHzyme). This unique CDs/LDHzyme demonstrate H2O2-responsive turn-on fluorescence via the self-regulating electron transfer process between Cu2+ and CDs. Furthermore, generated Cu+ through this electron transfer process can be beneficial for the POD-like activity, resulting an outstanding catalytic efficiency with the maximum initial velocity (Vmax ) as high as 2.002 × 10−7 M s−1, which is 229.9% higher than the natural peroxidase enzyme (Horseradish Peroxidase, HRP). Structure characterization and energy level calculation can further prove the mechanism of this self-regulating electron transfer process. These findings open an avenue to enable the utilization of nanozyme as a remedial nanoplatform to the selective fluorescence imaging and nanozyme-initiated chemodynamic therapy (NCDT).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tumor microenvironment
H2O2 sensing
LDHzyme
Carbon dots
Nanozyme-initiated chemodynamic therapy

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro FL imaging and enzyme dynamic therapy

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21905014, 22278031, 22288102). We also appreciate the support from Director Foundation of the Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences and the Youth Innovation Promotion Association of Chinese Academy of Sciences (No. 2019027).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic illustration of the construction procedure of CDs/LDHzyme. (B) TEM image of CDs (inset: HRTEM image of CDs and size distribution histogram). (C) AFM image of CDs (inset: height profiles of CDs). (D) XRD patterns of CDs and CuAl-LDH. (E) TEM image of CuAl-LDH. (F) AFM image of CuAl-LDH (inset: the thickness of CuAl-LDH monolayer nanosheets). (G) Surface zeta potentials of CDs, CuAl-LDH and CDs/LDHzyme in aqueous solution. (H) FT-IR spectra of CDs, CuAl-LDH and CDs/LDHzyme samples. (I) Thermogravimetric curves of CuAl-LDH and CDs/LDHzyme. (J) The size distribution of CDs/LDHzyme in water, DMEM, PBS within one week.

(A) FL spectra of CDs, CDs/LDHzyme and CDs/LDHzyme + H2O2. Time-dependent (0–20 min) FL spectra of (B) CDs and (C) CDs/LDHzyme (0.1 mg/mL) in PBS (pH 7.4) containing 200 μM H2O2, respectively. (D) Time-dependent (0–20 min) FL spectra of CDs/LDHzyme (0.1 mg/mL) containing 200 μM H2O2 in pH 6.5. (E) FL spectra of CDs/LDHzyme in the presence of different concentrations of H2O2 (0, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10, 15, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, and 350 μM). The inset illustrated the linear plot of the FL intensity ratio FR/FR0 vs the concentration of H2O2. FR and FR0 were the FL intensity of CDs/LDHzyme in the presence and absence of H2O2, respectively. (F) FL intensity of CDs/LDHzyme (0.1 mg/mL) with the interfering substances.

(A) UV–vis-NIR spectra of TMB reaction with different solutions. (B, C) Reaction of TMB with different concentrations of H2O2 at different pH values. (D) Comparison of the kinetic parameters of POD-like activity. (E) ESR spectrum of CDs/LDHzyme after reacting with H2O2 at different pH values. (F) Initial linear portion of reaction–time curves for CDs/LDHzyme. (G) Specific activities of CDs/LDHzyme. (H) Performance comparison of CDs, CuAl-LDH and CDs/LDHzyme.

High-resolution Cu 2p spectrum of (A) CuAl-LDH and (B) CDs/LDHzyme. (C) Cu LMM Auger lines of CuAl-LDH, CDs/LDHzyme and CDs/LDHzyme + H2O2. (D-F) High-resolution C 1 s spectrum of CDs, CDs/LDHzyme and CDs/LDHzyme + H2O2. Time-dependent (0–20 min) FL spectra of (G) CDs/MgAl-LDH + H2O2 (H) CDs/LDHzyme + EDTA in PBS (pH 7.4). (I) Band edge placement of CuAl-LDH and CDs. (J) Mechanism of FL quenching, recovery, and NCDT enhancement in CDs/LDHzyme.

(A) Confocal FL images of HeLa and PC12 cells with CDs/LDHzyme (100 μg mL−1) and H2O2 (100 μM) for 6 h. Scale bar = 20 μm. (B) Confocal FL images of HeLa cells incubated with CDs and CDs/LDHzyme (100 μg mL−1) for 6 h, respectively, and further incubated with different concentrations of H2O2 (0 μM, 50 μM, 100 μM) for 30 min. Scale bar = 50 μm. (C) Normalized mean fluorescence intensity of images. Relative viability of Hela cells incubated with (D) CDs and CDs/LDHzyme, with (E) CDs/LDHzyme and H2O2 (100 μM) at different pH values. (F-G) FL images of ROS and CA/PI production in HeLa cells. Data are represented as mean ± SD, n = 3, Student’s t-test, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of the CDs/LDHzyme nanozyme, together with their mechanism for ROS responsive FL imaging and NCDT.

head.ce:title: ROS-activated selective fluorescence imaging of cancer cells via the typical LDH-based nanozyme

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Qinghai University, Xining 810016, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Quzhou Institute for Innovation in Resource Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Quzhou Institute for Innovation in Resource Chemical Engineering, Quzhou, 324000, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Z. D. Wang and J. C. Liu contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The detection and treatment based on tumor microenvironment (TME) are essential in cancer therapy. Currently, nanomaterials with mimic-enzyme property have attracted tremendous interest and the regulation of the sensing and catalytical activity inside the living cells remains a challenge. Herein, a reactive oxygen species (ROS) driven nanozyme, with selective activated fluorescence imaging and multiple enzyme activity (including peroxidase, superoxide dismutase and catalase), was achieved via the confinement of CDs on the ultrathin CuAl-LDH (denoted as CDs/LDHzyme). This unique CDs/LDHzyme demonstrate H2O2-responsive turn-on fluorescence via the self-regulating electron transfer process between Cu2+ and CDs. Furthermore, generated Cu+ through this electron transfer process can be beneficial for the POD-like activity, resulting an outstanding catalytic efficiency with the maximum initial velocity (Vmax ) as high as 2.002 × 10−7 M s−1, which is 229.9% higher than the natural peroxidase enzyme (Horseradish Peroxidase, HRP). Structure characterization and energy level calculation can further prove the mechanism of this self-regulating electron transfer process. These findings open an avenue to enable the utilization of nanozyme as a remedial nanoplatform to the selective fluorescence imaging and nanozyme-initiated chemodynamic therapy (NCDT).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: The tumor microenvironment (TME) characterized by angiogenesis, acidosis, hypoxia and excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS), has been widely implicated in tumorigenesis [1]. Elevated level of H2O2 (a major kind of ROS) not only is an early marker of cancer, but also plays an important role in tumor therapy [2]. Fluorescent molecular probes have become an important tool for monitoring the unstable properties of H2O2 in cells [3], which can overcome the shortcomings of complex sample pretreatment meet the requirements of real-time or in-situ detection in vivo [4]. However, commonly used fluorescent molecular probes for H2O2 detection, such as aryl borate ester and diphenyl phosphine always suffer from poor stability, high toxicity, easy photobleaching and agglomeration [5]. Carbon dots (CDs), as an emerging carbon-based material, is possessed of various advantages such as good photostability, good biocompatibility, low toxicity, cheap raw material, and excellent water solubility, which can make it up to the traditional H2O2 detection probes [6]. Therefore, CDs can be considered as a good candidate for fluorescent indicators in actual sensors [7]. However, most reported CDs possess no specific responsive functionality, and CDs-based H2O2 detection strategies rely either on coupling specific recognition elements (such as antibodies and adaptors) to the synthetic CDs or on surface modification, followed by binding of the target analyte to induce the quenching of CDs fluorescence [8]. However, how to utilize the advantage of CDs to achieve the highly efficient H2O2-responsvie performance, remains a critical issue [9].

Nanozymes, with enzyme-like activity, improved stability, and better cost-effectiveness, can be easily modified and to adjust their activities [10]. Specifically, nanozymes have shown great promise in many reactions, such as redox reaction, hydrolysis, DNA cleavage, and especially ROS-related reactions [11]. H2O2 particularly, catalyzed by transition metal nanozymes with peroxidase (POD)-like activity, generates hydroxyl radicals (•OH) in acid condition via Fenton-like reaction [12], leading to the cell apoptosis. Since ROS levels in cancer cells can approach approximately 10 times higher than normal cells,[2,2,13] the POD-like nanozymes can be used to treat tumors, which is known as nanozyme-initiated chemodynamic therapy (NCDT)[13,14]. However, the highly complex TME leads to an insufficient catalytic efficiency of nanozymes and makes it difficult to achieve satisfactory reaction activity [10,13]. Hence, it remains a challenge to achieve highly efficient artificial enzymes that possess controllable catalytic activity in living cells [15]. Therefore, in view of the vital role of H2O2 in tumors, it is urgently needed to the combination of nanozymes with the fluorescence probe performance [3,16], which can make full use of the cellular microenvironment, and achieve biocatalytic transformations selectively in live cells.

As a two-dimensional layered nanozyme, layered double hydroxides (LDHs) is an excellent template for the construction of multifunctional system due to its adjustable geometric and electronic structure [17]. It should be note that the layers of LDH possess abundant hydroxyl groups [18], which is responsive to the weak acid environment [19]. Therefore, the utilization of LDH is more conducive to the stimulus response systems towards cancer therapy [18]. In combination with the characteristics of low toxicity and strong stability of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), an ideal platform for the sensing and treating cancer cells can be achieved at the same time [20]. Herein, we constructed an artificial nanozyme CDs/LDHzyme with enzyme-like activity for ROS-triggered turn on fluorescence imaging and NCDT. This CDs/LDHzyme showed excellent fluorescence sensing performance for H2O2. Meanwhile, the NCDT performance of CDs/LDHzyme was significantly enhanced compared with that of CuAl-LDH. The Vmax of CDs/LDHzyme is 1.818 × 10−7 M s−1 at pH 6.5. which is significantly stronger than CuAl-LDH (4.446 × 10−8 M s−1) and higher than natural POD enzyme (HRP). This unique ROS light-on mechanism was mainly via self-regulating electron transfer process between Cu2+ and CDs, which has been verified by both XPS and energy band structure analysis. This electron transfer mechanism overcomes the complex surface modification required by traditional CDs for fluorescence sensing, and exquisitely improves the performance of NCDT by using copper valence state transformation. In vitro cancer cell experiments also showed that CDs/LDHzyme had excellent performance in H2O2 fluorescence sensing imaging and NCDT. As a result, these findings may open the exploration and utilization of ROS-responsive LDHzyme as an advanced nanoplatform to distinguish cancer cells from normal cells based on the different concentrations of intracellular ROS and targeted therapy.

The preparation procedure of CDs/LDHzyme is illustrated in Scheme 1 and Fig. 1 A. Initially, the CDs were fabricated via a “bottom-up” hydrothermal method, and ultrathin LDHs a coprecipitation method, respectively [18]. The morphology was first investigated via transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As displayed in Fig. 1B, the obtained CDs demonstrate homogeneous sizes around 2.0 ± 0.5 nm with well-dispersed quasi-sphere structure. The high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image of CDs displays a lattice fringe of 0.201 nm (Fig. 1B, inset), which is assigned to the graphite (100) plane. The lateral size of the CuAl-LDH is approximately 65 ± 5 nm with a hexagonal morphology, and the lattice fringe spacing is around 0.157 nm, which can be attributed to the (1 1 0) plane. Subsequently, atomic force microscopy (AFM) analysis further determined that the thicknesses of the CDs and CuAl-LDH were respectively ∼ 2.1 nm and ∼ 1.14 nm. Next, CDs/LDHzyme was constructed by the confinement of CDs on CuAl-LDH nanosheet. The layered structure of CuAl-LDH was still maintained with the particle size around 75 nm (Figure S1), while the thickness of CDs/LDHzyme was around 3.40 nm (Figure S2). The particle sizes were further monitored by dynamic light scattering (DLS), the hydrodynamic diameter of CDs/LDHzyme was determined to be ∼ 84.8 nm, which is slightly higher than that of CuAl-LDH (∼74.7 nm, Figure S3). The successful synthesis of CDs/LDHzyme can also be confirmed by the X-ray diffractometry (XRD) analysis (Fig. 1D). The peak at 9.66° corresponds to the (001) crystal plane of graphite oxide, and the broad peak at 19.65° is consistent with the graphite (100) plane displayed in HR-TEM. The XRD analysis of CuAl-LDH revealed a diffraction peak at 12.07°, in accordance with the characteristic (003) reflection of the LDH phase. In the XRD pattern of CDs/LDHzyme, the peaks at 11.67° and 20.7° correspond to the graphite (100) plane and LDHs (003) plane, confirming the combination of two materials. Subsequently, Zeta potential analysis was carried out to investigate the surface conditions of CDs/LDHzyme. As shown in Fig. 1G, the CuAl-LDH present a positive zeta potential (+41.7 mV) in aqueous solution, while CDs shows a negative Zeta potential of − 26.1 mV. After loading CDs, the zeta potential of CDs/LDHzyme shifts to + 24.1 mV. This variation is resulted from the successful confinement of negatively-charged CDs on the positively-charged CuAl-LDH. In the FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1H), the characteristic absorption bands centered at 1384 cm−1 (symmetric stretching vibration of N–O) of NO3 − in LDHs and absorption band at 1520 cm−1 (skeleton vibration of benzene ring) of CDs were detected in the CDs/LDHzyme, indicating that CDs was successfully anchored onto CuAl-LDH. Moreover, the loading amounts of CDs was measured by thermogravimetry analysis (TG), which was calculated to be 10.1% according to the weight loss curve of CuAl-LDH and CDs/LDHzyme (Fig. 1I). After H2O2 treatment, some CDs fall off from the surface of CuAl-LDH (Figure S4). The particle size of CDs/LDHzyme was determined to be around 69 nm, which is also approximately equal to the original nanozyme. More importantly, CDs/LDHzyme exhibited high stability in various physiological environments, with an unchanged hydrodynamic diameter within one week (Fig. 1J).

The photophysical property and its stability are crucial for TME sensing and imaging. In determine whether this nanozyme can present ROS-activated fluorescence performance, we first conducted the UV − vis absorption spectra of various samples (Figure S5A). For CDs alone, two peaks centered at 236 and 283 nm are originated to the π − π* transitions of carbon in small aromatic rings and the n − π* transitions of C = O and C = N, while the band centered at 527 nm can be corresponded to π − π* transitions of larger conjugated structures formed in CDs. CDs/LDHzyme also have corresponding absorption peaks at 244 nm and 518 nm. Moreover, the FL spectra of CDs and CDs/LDHzyme can be excited in a wavelength-independent manner (Figure S5B, C). Typically, the maximum emission peak of CDs and CDs/LDHzyme can be observed at 590 nm, proving the nanozyme prepared can presents red fluorescence. Subsequently, the FL lifetime of CDs and CDs/LDHzyme were determined to be 1.45 and 2.63 ns, respectively, which is adequate for fluorescence imaging (Figure S6 and Table S1). Chemical stability is a key factor towards the cancer imaging, therefore, the chemical stability of CDs and CDs/LDHzyme were investigated under different concentrations of salt and broad pH ranges. As shown in Figure S7 and S8, the FL intensity at 590 nm of CDs and CDs/LDHzyme kept almost invariable in a wide salt concentration range (0–150 mM), which exhibites satisfactory resistance to salt solution and is beneficial for the biomedical applications. Meanwhile, the normalized FL intensity of CDs and CDs/LDHzyme only changed slightly under pH variation from 5.4 to 7.4.

To evaluate the sensing performance of CDs/LDHzyme on overexpressed H2O2 in TME, the responses of CDs/LDHzyme towards different concentrations of H2O2 are tested. As shown in Fig. 2 A, about 67% of the FL intensity of CDs at 590 nm is quenched after confined on CuAl-LDH nanosheet, while the quenched fluorescence can be dramatically recovered after introducing H2O2. Interestingly, the FL intensity of CDs had no significant change after adding 200 μM H2O2 for 20 min (Fig. 2B), while the FL intensity of CDs/LDHzyme gradually enhanced and stabilized around 20 min (Figure C). This phenomenon indicates that this designed CDs/LDHzyme has unique response toward H2O2 and potentials for selective fluorescence imaging of cancer cells. Moreover, as demonstrated in Fig. 2D and S9, the addition of H2O2 (200 μM) at pH 6.5 and 5.4 also causes evident FL enhancement over time, and reached a steady state around 20 min. These above results guarantee the fluorescent H2O2 sensing of CDs/LDHzyme in TME. Fig. 2E shows the FL spectra of CDs/LDHzyme in the presence of various concentrations of H2O2. The FL intensity of the system is gradually enhanced as the concentration of H2O2 increases, demonstrating that the correlation between the FL intensity and the H2O2 concentration. Meanwhile, the FL intensity presented fantastic linearity (R2 = 0.997) to H2O2 concentrations in the wide range of 0.25–350 μM (Fig. 2E, inset). The selectivity of CDs/LDHzyme was further investigated by adding various biologically relevant molecules, including ions (Na+, K+, Mg2+, and Ca2+), amino acids (lysine, glycine, arginine, and leucine), and glucose. As revealed in Fig. 2F, the CDs/LDHzyme possessed a remarkable specific response toward H2O2. The effect of superoxide anion on the fluorescence of CDs/LDHzyme was further investigated. The FL spectrum was illustrated in Figure S10, a modest fluorescence intensity increase can be observed after the adding of •O2 −. These results obtained by the proposed method indicated that CDs/LDHzyme possesses attractive sensitivity for H2O2, and holds potential biological applicability.

POD is acknowledged as an enzyme that can in situ catalyzes of endogenous H2O2 into highly toxic hydroxyl radicals (•OH) through the classic Fenton-like reaction. Encouraged by the unique electronic structure of CDs/LDHzyme with the doping of variable-valence Cu, it’s speculated that the CDs/LDHzyme can act as an effective POD-like nanozyme to catalyze H2O2 into •OH. In a standard procedure, 3,3,5,5-tetramethyl-benzidine (TMB) was selected as the chromogenic substrate to assess the POD-like activity. After the addition of the CDs/LDHzyme into H2O2 aqueous solution, the colorless TMB is oxidized by •OH into blue-colored oxidized TMB (ox-TMB, E = 3.9 × 104 M−1 cm−1) with a maximum characteristic absorbance at 652 nm. The absorption of the CDs/LDHzyme + H2O2 group has a noteworthy time-dependent augmentation (Figure S11), while the control groups without CDs/LDHzyme or H2O2 display inconspicuous color variation (Fig. 3 A). Furthermore, electron spin resonance (ESR) can be employed to determine the generation of •OH from CDs/LDHzyme and CuAl-LDH using 5,5-Dimethyl-1pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as a spin trap. As shown in Figure S12, the stronger •OH signals of the group treated with CDs/LDHzyme + H2O2 showed an apparent increase compared with that of the group incubated with CuAl-LDH + H2O2, indicating the enhanced POD-like activity of CDs/LDHzyme.

CDs/LDHzyme possesses similar pH-sensitive activities to natural POD. We quantified the capability of POD-like •OH generation by measuring the absorbance of the solution at 652 nm (Fig. 3B, C and S13). Acidic environment accelerates the speed of ROS generation and helps increase the amount of ROS. Michaelis Menten steady-state kinetics was used to assess the catalytic performance of CDs/LDHzyme. Different concentrations of H2O2 (i.e., 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2.5 and 5 μM) were mixed with 100 μg mL−1 CDs/LDHzyme for assay. A series of initial reaction rates at pH 6.5 were calculated and fitted by a Michaelis Menten curve via the Beer Lambert law (Figure S14A). The double reciprocal of the Michaelis Menten equation was obtained by Equation (1) as shown in Figure S14B.
(1)
1
V 0
=
K m
V
max
×
1
[ S ]
+
1
V
max

The Michaelis Menten constant (KM ) and maximum velocity (Vmax ) were calculated to be 7.31 mM and 1.818 × 10−7 M·s−1, respectively. At pH 5.4, the KM and Vmax values obtained are 4.208 mM and 2.002 × 10−7 M·s−1 (Figure S14C, D). The obtained KM value is intermediate while the Vmax is relatively high compared with previous reports and HRP (Table S2), suggesting an outstanding substrate affinity and Fenton-like activity of CDs/LDHzyme. Moreover, the Vmax values of CDs/LDHzyme were higher than those of CuAl-LDH, while the KM values were lower than those of CuAl-LDH, when the substrate is H2O2 and TMB (Fig. 3D, S15 and S16). The elevated Vmax and reduced KM suggest that charge transfer between CDs and CuAl-LDH may enhance the NCDT performance of CDs/LDHzyme. Subsequently, ESR was also used to explore the existence of •OH in different pH environments (Fig. 3E), and DMPO was used as the capture agent. ESR signals could barely be observed for CDs/LDHzyme + H2O2 at neutral pH conditions (pH 7.4), while increased ESR signal intensity was noted in mildly acidic conditions (pH 6.5 and 5.4). The specific activity value (U per milligram) of CDs/LDHzyme were further quantitatively evaluated. The slopes of the initial linear part of the reaction curve for CDs/LDHzyme at different concentrations were calculated and plotted with the corresponding mass, and the specific activity value was calculated as 0.0501 U/mg (Fig. 3F, G). These results demonstrated that CDs/LDHzyme possess not only H2O2 sensing ability, but also significantly enhanced POD-like activity (Fig. 3H), indicating that this fabricated nanozyme has potentials for selective fluorescence imaging of cancer cells and enzyme dynamic therapy.

Furthermore, the CDs/LDHzyme was assessed for its ability to mimic other redox enzymes. As shown in Figure S17, although the CDs/LDHzyme exhibited negligible oxidase (OXD)-like and glucose oxidase (GOD)-like activities, the catalase (CAT)-like and superoxide dismutase (SOD)-like activities are rather satisfactory. The catalase (CAT)-like activity of CDs/LDHzyme was measured based on its ability to splits H2O2 into O2 and water. As shown in Figure S17C, the decomposition of H2O2 by CDs/LDHzyme followed a Michaelis–Menten kinetics. The Vmax of CDs/LDHzyme showed 3.71-fold decrease relative to CuAl-LDH (Table S3), which can effectively reduce the consumption of H2O2 and is more conducive to NCDT. Then, the superoxide dismutase (SOD)-like activity investigation of CDs/LDHzyme was executed with reference to nitrotetrazolium blue chloride via a xanthine-xanthine oxidase reaction in a colorimetric manner at Abs560 nm. As shown in Figure S17D, the inhibition rate was 90.0% when the CDs/LDHzyme concentration was 100 μg/mL. In contrast, the CuAl-LDH showed weak SOD-like activity, indicating that electron transfer between CDs and CuAl-LDH was critical to the catalytic activity of CDs/LDHzyme. In CuAl-LDH, Cu exists at + 2 valence, while in CDs/LDHzyme, the electron transfer between Cu and CDs leads to the valence transition of Cu. We speculate that it is due to the existence of mixed-valence states of Cu2+ and Cu+ that CDs/LDHzyme displays multiple enzyme-like catalytic activities [21]. More importantly, the H2O2 converted from ·O2 − by SOD-like activity of CDs/LDHzyme can continuously replenish H2O2 in situ of tumor, and H2O2 can further react with CDs/LDHzyme to produce •OH, which promotes the efficacy of NCDT.

To understand the mechanism underlying the fluorescent responding and enhanced NCDT performances based on the CDs/LDHzyme, the chemical state of different elements in CuAl-LDH, CDs and CDs/LDHzyme were firstly analyzed via XPS. The survey spectrum exhibits seven characteristic peaks of Cu 2p (934.5 eV), O 1 s (531.6 eV), N 1 s (399.4 eV), C 1 s (285.3 eV), S 2 s (231.6 eV), S 2p (168.6 eV) and Al 2p (78.4 eV) in the CDs/LDHzyme (Figure S18C). Compared with CDs, the XPS spectrum of CDs/LDHzyme exhibited new peaks at 934.5 eV and 78.4 eV (Figure S18), attributed to the Cu 2p and Al 2p bands, further confirming the successfully confinement of CDs. Specifically, high-resolution XPS spectra obtained from the Cu 2p scan of CuAl-LDH reveals two dominant peaks at 934.9 and 954.9 eV, assigning to Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2, respectively (Fig. 4 A). Besides, satellite peaks at the range of 938–948 eV can also be observed, verifying the existence of Cu2+ in the CuAl-LDH. As shown in Fig. 4B, low valence state of Cu occurred in high-resolution XPS spectra for CDs/LDHzyme. By examining the Cu LMM Auger spectra, the peak of CDs/LDHzyme deviated to the direction of high energy field compared with CuAl-LDH, which also indicated the presence of Cu+ in CDs/LDHzyme (Fig. 4C). It is speculated that the appearance of Cu+ is due to the electron transfer between CuAl-LDH and CDs. To further verify this speculation, the valence states of C in CDs and CDs/LDHzyme were also analyzed. The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum of CDs can be well-fitted into five curves: the binding energies at 288.1 eV and 286.2 eV are ascribed to the C=O and C − O groups, respectively; the peak at 285.2 eV is assigned to C − N; the peak at 284.6 eV suggests the presence of C–C, and the peak at 284.2 eV is attributed to C=C (Fig. 4D). Interestingly, the integral peak area of C = O in CDs accounts for 3.62%, while the ratio of which in CDs/LDHzyme is 12.58%. The overall binding energy of C 1 s shifts from 285.01 eV to 285.72 eV, indicating that the carbon groups in CDs/LDHzyme is oxidized (Fig. 4D, 4E). According to the XPS spectra of Cu and C, electron-deficient Cu2+ has a high binding affinity for the electron-rich carbon groups on the surface of CDs, resulting the generation of abundant Cu+, and the peroxidase-mimicking activity of CDs/LDHzyme was significantly improved compared with CuAl-LDH. Meanwhile, the charge transfer between Cu2+ and CDs disturbs the initial electron transition of CDs and facilitating the transfer of excited electrons on CDs to the unfilled orbital of Cu2+. The resulting electron transfer process leads to the obvious FL quenching of CDs. As shown in Fig. 4C and S19, after the addition of H2O2, the proportion of Cu+ decreased prominently due to the Fenton-like reaction between Cu+ and H2O2. And the integral peak area ratio of C = O in CDs also recovered to a lower level (Fig. 4F). In addition, the change of Cu2+ valence state into Cu+, which is more favorable to POD reaction, so that the NCDT of the CDs/LDHzyme is significantly enhanced.

As for the fluorescence process of CDs/LDHzyme, the existence of Cu disrupts the initial in-situ electron transition of CDs and facilitates the transfer of excited electrons on CDs to the unfilled orbital of Cu2+, leading to the obvious FL quenching of CDs. Control samples and energy level calculations were introduced to further prove the fluorescence quenching and enhancing mechanism of inner electron transition. Control experiment was carried out using MgAl-LDH to further study the relation of FL quenching and enhancing to Cu2+ in CuAl-LDH. It was found that the FL intensity of CDs/MgAl-LDH without Cu2+ was very stable after adding H2O2, similar to that of original CDs (Fig. 4G), proving that the Cu2+ plays an important role in the selective FL response of the CDs/LDHzyme to the H2O2. The quenched FL can be recovered by adding a strong metal ion chelator ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA; Fig. 4H), which further proved the Cu2+ quenching and recovering mechanism.

The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy levels of CDs were introduced to confirm the electron transfer occurred during the FL process, which is studied by cyclic voltammetry via a standard three electrode system. The HOMO and LUMO energy of CDs were calculated to be − 4.63 and − 2.51 eV (Figure S20A), respectively. The bandgap of 2.12 eV is very close to the absorption edge of CDs at 2.07 eV (Figure S20B). The HOMO and LUMO energy of CuAl-LDH were also detected to be − 4.36 and − 2.57 eV with an energy gap of 1.79 eV based on the cyclic voltammetry curve (Figure S20C). By comparing the band edge placement of CDs and CuAl-LDH, a mechanism for the quenched FL and enhanced catalytic efficiency in the CDs/LDHzyme system was proposed in Fig. 4I and 4 J. On the ultrathin CuAl-LDH layer, electron transfer between electron-deficient Cu2+ and photoexcited CDs results the fluorescence quenching of CDs. After the CDs/LDHzyme is treated with H2O2, the electron transfer between CuAl-LDH and CDs is partially blocked by H2O2 and some CDs fall off the LDH host layer, which is agree with the observed TEM results. These results lead to the recovery of fluorescence of the CDs. This electron transfer strategy gives CDs/LDHzyme excellent fluorescence response to H2O2 and enables it to have superior NCDT performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro FL imaging and enzyme dynamic therapy
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the excellent FL behavior and the unique ROS stimuli-responsive properties of CDs/LDHzyme, their feasibility for selective ROS-activated FL imaging was probed at cellular level. Initially, the HeLa and PC12 cells are treated only with CDs/LDHzyme for 30 min. HeLa cells is a typical cervical cancer cell, while PC12 is a normal neuroendocrine cell used as a comparison. As demonstrated in Fig. 5 A, weak fluorescence signal can be detected in both control and PC12 groups, which indicates that relatively low level of H2O2 in the HeLa and PC12 cells. In contrast, after co-incubation with H2O2, fluorescence intensity in HeLa cells was significantly enhanced, indicating that CDs/LDHzyme was sensitive to exogenous H2O2. In order to study the effect of endogenous H2O2 on CDs/LDHzyme fluorescence, the HeLa cells were pretreated with phorbol-12-myristate-13-acetate (PMA) (promote cell endogenous H2O2 generation) for 1 h and then incubated with a CDs/LDHzyme for 4 h. Compared with the untreated cells, the PMA-pretreated cells exhibited a significant increase in the fluorescent signal (Figure S21). The results demonstrate that CDs/LDHzyme could image endogenous H2O2 in living HeLa cells. Meanwhile, we used CDs/LDHzyme to incubate with HeLa cells alone, and detected its fluorescence position in Hela cells. As indicated in Figure S22, the results revealed that CDs/LDHzyme is mainly located in the cytoplasm. After being washed three times with pre-cooled PBS buffer, HeLa cells were incubated with CDs and CDs/LDHzyme respectively, and then different concentrations of H2O2 were added (Fig. 5B). With the increased concentration of H2O2, the fluorescence intensity in the red channel of CDs/LDHzyme group was gradually enhanced, while that in the CDs group barely changed (Fig. 5C). Therefore, CDs/LDHzyme exhibited splendid biocompatibility and cell permeability. The variation of H2O2 level in living cells can reflect in the fluorescence intensity change in the red channel. Taken together, these results reveal that our artificial enzyme system possessed great potential to evaluate excess H2O2 in cancer cells.

Furthermore, the characteristic cell cytotoxicity of CDs and CDs/LDHzyme were evaluated with HeLa cells as model cell line using a cell counting kit-8 (CCK8) assay. Initially, we have investigated the relative viabilities of HeLa cells after incubation with different concentrations of H2O2 (Figure S23). The survival rate of HeLa cells was higher than 93% upon the addition of H2O2, even at 100 μM. Then, the biocompatibility of CDs and CDs/LDHzyme were investigated. More than 85% cells can survive after the incubation with CDs and CDs/LDHzyme at a high concentration of 100 μg mL−1 for 24 h, indicating that the samples present good biocompatibility (Fig. 5D).

Subsequently, in vitro NCDT induced by CDs/LDHzyme was investigated. After incubation with of CDs/LDHzyme (100 μg mL−1) and H2O2 for 24 h, the cell viabilities can reach 84.93% at pH 7.4, while the cell viability dramatically decreased to 9.73% at pH 6.5 (Fig. 5E). These results demonstrate this nanozyme is very responsive to the weak acidic TME. The NCDT performance in the tumor microenvironment was further verified via the intracellular ROS probe 2, 7-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA). Compared with the control group, HeLa cells incubated with CDs/LDHzyme present a weak green fluorescence signal at pH 7.4. Notably enhanced green emission was observed at pH 6.5, demonstrating that a large amount of intracellular •OH was produced (Fig. 5F, S24). Then, HeLa cells treated with saline, and CDs/LDHzyme in different pH were stained with calcein-AM (green) and propidium iodide-PI (red). As observed in Fig. 5G, cell viability was not affected in the control group, as demonstrated by the strong green fluorescence, which represented living cells. However, cell death was increased in the CDs/LDHzyme and H2O2 (pH 7.4) group, and was further exacerbated at pH 6.5. Furthermore, we investigated the flow cytometry in Figure S25. The viability of the CDs/LDHzyme group remained over 90%, which is consistence with the CCK8 result. After the introduction of H2O2, the viability decreased obviously, which was caused by the generation of •OH. The results showed that CDs/LDHzyme had TME-responsive NCDT performance, which was consistent with the above cytotoxicity data. This unique TME-response was attributed to CDs/LDHzyme acid enhanced Fenton-like reaction, which effectively reduced the damage to normal cells.

In order to investigate the biocompatibility of CDs/LDHzyme, we have i.v. injected this CDs/LDHzyme and followed by the sacrificing the mouse after 7 days. Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining was employed to evaluate the toxicity of the CDs/LDHzyme. H&E staining images of major organs revealed no obvious tissue damage can be found (Figure S26), which can confirm the good biocompatibility of this CDs/LDHzyme. To further investigate the in vivo FL imaging capability of CDs/LDHzyme, we have intratumorally injected the CDs/LDHzyme into xenograft tumor-bearing mice. As presented in Figure S27, intense FL signal is detected from tumors after the injection of CDs/LDHzyme, which revealed that our artificial enzyme system possessed great potential for in vivo FL imaging.

In summary, we have successfully constructed an artificial nanozyme CDs/LDHzyme via a self-regulating electron transfer process. This nanozyme is responsive to intracellular ROS, and selectively lighten the tumor cell with enhanced NCDT performance. It is verified that the electron transfer between CDs and Cu2+ on LDH layer fluorescence quenching, and changes Cu2+ to Cu+, which is also favorable for POD-like reaction. And CDs/LDHzyme demonstrated excellent turn-on fluorescence sensing performance for H2O2, which can distinguish cancer cells from normal cells. Moreover, the Vmax of CDs/LDHzyme was higher than that of native POD enzyme and original LDHzyme due to the production of Cu+. In vitro cell experiments also showed that CDs/LDHzyme had excellent performance in H2O2 fluorescence sensing imaging and NCDT treatment, which extends their biomedical use from traditional catalysis, energy storage to sensor and enzyme dynamic therapy. Taken together, our LDH-based nanozyme is acknowledged as a potential nanozyme for the specific utilization in selective fluorescence imaging and NCDT.

Preparation of CDs: 2,5-Diaminobenzenesulfonic acid (100.0 mg) was dissolved in 10 mL of deionized (DI) water. Then the solution was transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-equipped stainless autoclave for heating 8 h at 200 °C. The obtained solution was purified with a dialysis bag (molecular weight cutoff 1 kDa) for 24 h to dialyze the nonreactive molecules. Finally, the solid-state CDs were gained by freeze-drying.

Preparation of CuAl-LDH: Firstly, Cu(NO3)2·6H2O (0.6 mmol) and Fe(NO3)3·9H2O (0.3 mmol) were dissolved in DI water (30 mL) as solution A. Secondly, NaOH (0.275 mmol) was dissolved in another 30 mL DI water to obtain solution B. Afterwards, NaNO3 (0.15 mmol) was dissolved in deionized water containing 25% formamide (30 mL) to prepare solution C. At room temperature, solution A and B were slowly added to solution C, with the pH value adjusted to 8.0 ± 0.3. Meanwhile, nitrogen (N2) was injected into the drip process and stirred at room temperature for 1 h. Thirdly, the synthetic LDHs colloid was centrifuged three times with DI water and ethanol at 7000 rpm. Finally, dialysis was performed for 48 h to remove residual formamide, and the obtained CuAl-LDH were stored in the DI water for further use.

Preparation of CDs/LDHzyme: CDs (10 mg) was added into the CuAl-LDH solution with ultrasonication for 30 min and then stirred at 800 rpm for 4 h. The CDs/LDHzyme were collected by centrifugation at 7000 rpm for 10 min and washed with DI water and ethanol three times.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21905014, 22278031, 22288102). We also appreciate the support from Director Foundation of the Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences and the Youth Innovation Promotion Association of Chinese Academy of Sciences (No. 2019027).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.144020.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
